ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Sup Bebola Daging; closest defensible match is 'beef soup' (FOODON_03315595). Meatball-specific and Malay-specific variants are not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Beef soup) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific code exists for Malay-style meatball beef soup in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Sup Bebola Daging or generic beef meatball soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sup Bebola Daging is approximately 350 ml (350 g), typically served as a main dish alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Malaysian Foods — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central (Beef soup, SR Legacy) for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia; FAO/INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Sup Bebola Daging specifically. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for Malay beef soup and beef meatball dishes, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Beef soup, home-prepared, FDC ID 172166) for micronutrient completeness. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with these references and have been retained where corroborated. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef soup data (~1.3 g/100 g). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available at this level of specificity in approved sources; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on beef-and-vegetable soup with low-GI vegetables (carrots, potatoes in small quantity) and high protein content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Sup Bebola Daging in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 (low) is estimated by analogy with beef-and-vegetable soups documented in Atkinson et al. (2021) and the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values of 30–40 for protein-rich, low-carbohydrate meat soups. The dish contains 4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from potatoes and carrots. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 14.0) / 100 = 4.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — 250 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 875 mg sodium, representing ~38% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 ml, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid adding additional soy sauce or salt at the table to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (4 g per 100 g), with high protein (7.5 g per 100 g) that supports glycemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sup Bebola Daging is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; monitor potato quantity as it contributes the majority of the carbohydrate load, and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice to further reduce postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.3 g per 100 g) from beef and elevated sodium (250 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors in this dish.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef cuts, skim visible fat from the broth, limit serving size to 250 ml, and avoid high-sodium accompaniments to reduce cumulative saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, star anise, cinnamon) and zinc (1.8 mg per 100 g) and iron (1.2 mg per 100 g) from beef may support joint health.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Generally suitable; individuals who identify red meat or specific spices as personal inflammatory triggers should monitor symptoms and adjust portion or ingredient selection accordingly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sup Bebola Daging is a traditional Malay beef meatball soup widely consumed in Malaysia and among Malay communities in Singapore and Brunei. The dish features bite-sized beef meatballs simmered in a clear, aromatic broth seasoned with whole spices including star anise, cinnamon sticks, and cloves, which impart a warm, subtly sweet depth of flavour. The broth is further built with ginger, garlic, and shallots, producing a fragrant and comforting soup. Potatoes and carrots are standard additions, contributing mild sweetness and body to the dish. Sup Bebola Daging is typically served hot alongside steamed white rice and garnished with fried shallots, sliced fresh chillies, and Chinese celery (daun sup), which add textural contrast and brightness. The soup is a staple at Malay family meals, festive occasions, and warung (roadside stalls) throughout Malaysia. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 263 kcal, 26 g protein, 11 g fat, and 14 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in protein, moderate in fat, and low in carbohydrates, with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is notable at 250 mg per 100 g (approximately 875 mg per serving), which warrants attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The beef provides meaningful quantities of zinc (1.8 mg/100 g), iron (1.2 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (0.8 mcg/100 g), and niacin (3.5 mg/100 g). The spice blend contributes antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is naturally halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is beef meatball soup suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of Sup Bebola Daging
What spices are in Malay beef soup?
Glycemic index of Malay beef soup
Can I eat Sup Bebola Daging if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sup Bebola Daging as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Malay beef soup entries and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef soup data. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with these references. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is therefore rated medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for Sup Bebola Daging as a composite Malay dish are not available at the required specificity in IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. Saturated fat (1.3 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef soup (FDC ID 172166); this is an adaptation and not an exact match. 3. Input sources (Malaysian Journal of Public Health Medicine, Malaysian Dietitians' Association) are not on the approved source list and have not been used as primary evidence. 4. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical '1 serving (350ml)' entries) has been deduplicated to a single 350 g serving. 5. Vitamin A value (85 mcg RAE/100 g) is plausible given carrot content; retained from input with medium confidence. 6. This dish is a Malay dish originating in Malaysia; multilingual requirements apply for Malay only (not Singaporean dish multilingual matrix). Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Malaysian Malay dish and are set to null.
